Documentation of health algorithms for step counting and sleep tracking using accelerometer data and VMC values.

# Purpose
The document describes the implementation and integration of health algorithms for step counting and sleep tracking in a wearable device. The step counting algorithm uses accelerometer data to detect walking patterns and count steps by analyzing the frequency of footfall signals through Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and Vector Magnitude Counts (VMC). The sleep tracking algorithm determines sleep and restful sleep periods using minute-level VMC values and average orientation data. The document details the system integration of these algorithms within the firmware, highlighting the roles of various modules such as `activity.c`, `activity_insights.c`, and the Kraepelin algorithm modules. It also outlines the testing procedures for these algorithms, including the use of unit tests and sample data to verify the accuracy of step and sleep detection. The document provides a comprehensive overview of the technical implementation, data processing, and testing methodologies used to ensure the functionality and reliability of the health tracking features.
# Content Summary
The document describes the implementation and functionality of health algorithms used in a wearable device, specifically focusing on step counting and sleep tracking. The step counting algorithm utilizes accelerometer data to detect user movement and count steps. It processes accelerometer readings in 5-second epochs, applies a Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to determine the energy of signals at different frequencies, and uses heuristics to identify the footfall signal, which is the most reliable indicator of steps. The algorithm also incorporates Vector Magnitude Counts (VMC) to measure overall movement and refine step detection by considering harmonics and distinguishing between arm swings and actual steps.

The sleep tracking algorithm uses minute-level VMC values and average orientation data to determine sleep patterns. It identifies sleep sessions by analyzing periods of low movement and orientation stability. The algorithm distinguishes between sleep and non-worn states by checking for consistent low VMC values and unchanged orientation. It also identifies restful sleep periods within sleep sessions based on lower thresholds for movement.

The document outlines the integration of these algorithms into the firmware, detailing the code organization and the roles of various modules. The `activity.c` module serves as the main interface for the activity service, while the `kraepelin` directory contains the core algorithm code. The algorithms are tested using unit tests that process captured sample data to verify expected outcomes. The document also describes tools and methods for capturing and analyzing accelerometer and VMC data for testing purposes.
